subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) WDDX serialization extension. WddxPacket::recursiveAddVar is the recursive serializer that turns a PHP userspace value (scalar, array, or object) into a WDDX XML packet by appending markup fragments to the member accumulator m_packetString. WDDX is an XML-based data-interchange serialization format; this routine emits <var>, <struct>, <array>, and <string> elements describing the PHP value graph.
provenance: Identified from the type vocabulary and method names, which are HHVM/PHP runtime idioms: HHVM's tagged-value types String / Variant / Array / Object / ArrayIter, the m_packetString accumulator on a WddxPacket class, Variant::toObject()/toArray()/toBoolean(), ObjectData::o_getClassName(), and the WDDX-specific helpers getDataTypeString() and getWddxEncoded(). The literal WDDX element names (<struct>, <array length='...'>, <var name='...'>, php_class_name) are the WDDX wire vocabulary as implemented by the PHP ext/wddx serializer that HHVM re-implements. The sample tag comment /* sample: primevul_train_2079_vuln */ and filename train_0115_cwe79.cpp indicate a CWE-79 (improper output neutralization) case.
trust_boundary: The two data inputs varName and varVariant are PHP userspace values reaching the serializer through wddx_serialize_value / wddx_serialize_vars / wddx_add_vars. In a web application these values (array keys, string/scalar values, and object class names) are frequently attacker-controllable: request parameters placed into arrays, user objects, or dynamically-named keys. The generated m_packetString is untrusted-derived XML that is subsequently stored, transmitted, or rendered. The trust boundary is therefore the point where these userspace bytes are concatenated verbatim into XML markup: any byte the attacker can place in a key name, class name, or value crosses into a markup context and must be neutralized before emission.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The method signature. recursiveAddVar is a member of WddxPacket that serializes one named value into the packet's XML accumulator. varName is the name to place in the <var name='...'> attribute (an HHVM String passed by const reference, so its lifetime is the caller's). varVariant is the value to serialize (an HHVM Variant, the tagged-union PHP value, by const reference). hasVarTag controls whether this value should be wrapped in a <var name='...'>...</var> element (true at top level and for struct members, false for bare array elements). It returns bool indicating whether it emitted anything.

what_the_code_does.code:
bool isArray = varVariant.isArray();
  bool isObject = varVariant.isObject();
what_the_code_does.explanation: Classifies the value. isArray is true if the Variant holds a PHP array; isObject is true if it holds an object instance. These two flags drive whether the value is serialized as a composite (<array> / <struct>) or as a scalar via the fall-through path at the bottom of the function.

The composite branch. When the value is an array or object and hasVarTag is set, it opens a WDDX <var> element whose name attribute is the variable name. Critically, varName.data() (the raw C-string bytes of the HHVM String) is concatenated directly between the single-quoted attribute delimiters with no escaping or neutralization of XML-significant characters. This is the primary output-encoding sink for the composite path.

Materializes the composite into an iterable Array. varAsObject holds the object view (used later to fetch the class name). If the value is a native array it is taken directly; if it is an object, its property set is coerced to an array via Object::toArray() so the code can iterate object properties as key/value pairs. Both objects and arrays are thereby normalized to varAsArray for the element loop.

length is the element count of the normalized array. The non-empty branch begins by peeking at the first key with an ArrayIter: if the first key is a string, isObject is forced true, meaning a PHP array with string keys is serialized as a WDDX <struct> rather than an <array>. This reclassification is a heuristic: string-keyed arrays cannot be represented as positional WDDX arrays, so they are promoted to struct form.

Emits the container header. For struct/object output it writes <struct>, and if the value is a genuine object (not merely a string-keyed array), it also emits a synthetic php_class_name member whose <string> body is the class name obtained from varAsObject->o_getClassName().c_str() — again concatenated verbatim with no escaping. For a positional array it writes <array length='N'> where N comes from std::to_string(length), an integer conversion that produces only digits and is therefore not an injection sink.

The recursion. It walks every element of the normalized array, and for each element calls recursiveAddVar with the element's key coerced to a String as the new varName, the element value as the new varVariant, and isObject as the new hasVarTag (so struct members get <var> wrappers, positional array members do not). This is where nested arrays/objects drive unbounded recursion depth equal to the nesting depth of the input value graph.
Closes the container element opened at lines 25-36, matching the struct/array choice made earlier so the emitted XML nests correctly. Static markup only; no untrusted data here.

The length == 0 branch, handling an empty object (an object with no properties). It emits an empty <struct> and, for a real object, the same synthetic php_class_name member. Note this duplicates the class-name sink at line 55 (identical to line 29): the class name is concatenated into the <string> body verbatim. An empty native array (isObject false) emits nothing here.
Closes the outer <var> wrapper opened at lines 10-14 when hasVarTag was set, then returns true to signal that the composite was serialized. This ends the isArray || isObject branch.

The scalar path, reached when the value is neither array nor object. varType is the WDDX type name derived from the Variant's runtime type (e.g. "string", "number", "boolean"), copied into a std::string from getDataTypeString(...).data(). The guard getWddxEncoded(varType, "", varName, false).empty() probes whether this type is serializable at all. varValue is the string form of the value (varVariant.toString().data()), specially remapped to the literal "true"/"false" for booleans. The actual emission is delegated to getWddxEncoded(varType, varValue, varName, hasVarTag), whose implementation is not shown — so whether the scalar value and varName are XML-escaped depends entirely on that helper, unlike the composite path which escapes nothing.
Fall-through return when the value is a scalar whose type getWddxEncoded reports as non-serializable (the guard at line 68 was false). Nothing was appended and the caller is told nothing was emitted.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Markup/XSS injection via an unescaped variable name. varName is an attacker-influenceable value: it originates as an array key coerced to a String at line 40 (recursiveAddVar(key.toString(), ...)) and PHP array keys can contain arbitrary bytes including ', <, >, and &. Because the raw bytes are concatenated directly inside the single-quoted name attribute with no neutralization, a key such as x'><script>alert(1)</script><var name='y closes the attribute and the <var> element and injects arbitrary markup/script into the WDDX packet. When that packet is later stored, transmitted, or rendered in an HTML/XML context, the injected content executes or corrupts the document (CWE-79 / CWE-116). The implementation must XML-entity-encode ', ", <, >, & in varName before appending — the invariant is that no userspace byte may reach a markup delimiter position unescaped.

Second injection sink: the object's class name is written verbatim into the <string> element body with no escaping. PHP class names are normally restricted, but anonymous classes, closures, and internally-constructed class-name strings can contain characters such as \ and, in some runtimes, arbitrary bytes; more importantly the & and < characters break XML even without an attack. Any <, >, & in the class name corrupts or escapes the <string> context, and if the packet is rendered as HTML the payload becomes stored XSS (CWE-79 / CWE-116). This exact sink is duplicated at line 55 in the empty-object branch, so both code paths must escape the class name identically.

The empty-object duplicate of the class-name sink. It has the same defect as lines 28-30: an unescaped class name in the <string> body. It is called out separately because a fix applied only to the non-empty path (line 29) would leave this empty-object path (line 55) still injectable, so the escaping remediation must cover both.

Uncontrolled recursion. recursiveAddVar recurses once per level of array/object nesting with no depth counter or limit. An attacker who supplies a deeply nested value graph (trivial to build: an array nested thousands of levels deep, or a self-referential structure that toArray() expands) drives recursion depth proportional to input nesting, exhausting the C++ call stack and crashing the process (denial of service, CWE-674). The implementation must enforce a maximum recursion/serialization depth and reject inputs that exceed it.

Latent encoding dependency in the scalar path. Unlike the composite path (which demonstrably escapes nothing), the scalar path routes both varValue and varName through getWddxEncoded, whose body is not shown. If getWddxEncoded fails to XML-escape the value bytes and the name, this becomes a third injection sink for attacker-controlled scalar strings and their names. Given that the composite path in the same function performs no escaping at all, the safe assumption is that this helper is the ONLY place escaping could occur, and it must be verified: the invariant is that getWddxEncoded neutralizes ', ", <, >, & in every field it emits. This is a latent finding whose bug lives in the not-provided implementation of getWddxEncoded.

Temporary-lifetime pattern worth confirming but not a defect here. getDataTypeString(...) returns an HHVM String temporary and .data() yields a pointer into that temporary's buffer; likewise line 69 does varVariant.toString().data(). Because the result is immediately used to copy-construct a std::string within the same full expression — the temporary is guaranteed alive until the end of the statement — the bytes are copied before the String is destroyed, so there is no use-after-free (contrast CWE-416, which would arise if the raw pointer were stored or the String were a shorter-lived subexpression). The invariant to keep is that these .data()/.c_str() results are consumed within the full expression and never stored.

summary: This WDDX serializer builds an XML packet by concatenating attacker-influenceable variable names (line 12) and object class names (lines 29, 55) directly into markup with zero output escaping, an improper-neutralization / stored-XSS defect (CWE-79/CWE-116) that lets crafted array keys or class names inject arbitrary XML/script into the packet. Secondary risks are uncontrolled recursion over nested input (CWE-674, DoS) and a scalar path whose safety hinges entirely on the unshown getWddxEncoded escaping correctly.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: recursiveAddVar(const String& varName, const Variant& varVariant, bool hasVarTag) signature (lines 2-4)
reviewed_anchors.reason: varName and varVariant are userspace, attacker-influenceable inputs concatenated into XML markup; the function is the injection surface.
Local boolean classification of the value type; no untrusted byte reaches an output context through them.
Attacker-controlled variable name written verbatim into a single-quoted XML attribute with no escaping — primary XSS/markup injection sink.
Type coercion to a uniform iterable; produces no output and imposes no unescaped-copy contract.
Element count used only for a loop bound and for std::to_string; not attacker-chosen bytes and not concatenated as raw markup.
Heuristic reclassification of string-keyed arrays to struct form; a logic choice, not a memory- or injection-safety contract.
Class name written verbatim into a <string> body with no escaping — second XSS/markup injection sink.
Integer-to-decimal conversion yields only digits; cannot carry markup-significant characters.
Self-recursion with no depth bound; nested attacker input causes stack exhaustion (CWE-674).
Duplicate unescaped class-name sink on the length==0 branch; must be fixed alongside line 29.

The .data() pointer from a temporary String is copied into std::string within the same full expression, so no use-after-free; varType is a controlled type name (string/number/boolean), not raw attacker bytes.
Serializability probe on the empty string; not itself an output sink, though its sibling emission at line 73 is reviewed.
Same temporary-lifetime pattern as line 67, copied in-expression; the value's escaping is handled (or not) inside getWddxEncoded, covered by the line 73 anchor.
Scalar emission whose XSS-safety depends entirely on the unshown getWddxEncoded escaping varValue and varName — latent finding to confirm.

findings.title: Stored XSS / XML injection via unescaped WDDX variable name
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of special elements in output (markup/XSS injection)
findings.invariant: Every byte of varName placed inside the single-quoted name attribute must be XML-entity-encoded (', ", <, >, & at minimum) so no userspace byte can reach a markup-delimiter position.
findings.failure_mode: varName comes from an array key coerced to String (line 40) and is attacker-controllable. A key like x'><script>...</script><var name='y closes the attribute and the <var> element and injects arbitrary markup; when the resulting WDDX packet is rendered in an HTML/XML context the payload executes (stored XSS) or corrupts the document.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no escaping is applied to varName on the composite path and that WDDX packets flow to a context (browser, XML parser, storage) where injected markup is honored.

findings.title: Stored XSS / XML injection via unescaped object class name
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of special elements in output (markup/XSS injection)
findings.invariant: The class-name string must be XML-escaped before being written into the <string> element body; <, >, & must never appear raw.
findings.failure_mode: The class name (anonymous classes, closures, or attacker-constructed class strings) is concatenated verbatim into the <string> body. Any <, >, & corrupts the XML, and in an HTML rendering context injected tags become stored XSS. The identical sink is duplicated at line 55, so both paths are affected.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify o_getClassName() output is not escaped here and that the same fix is applied to the duplicate sink at line 55.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion over nested input causes stack exhaustion
findings.invariant: Serialization depth must be bounded by a configurable maximum, and inputs exceeding it must be rejected rather than recursed into.
findings.failure_mode: recursiveAddVar recurses once per nesting level with no depth guard. A deeply nested array/object supplied by the attacker drives recursion depth proportional to input nesting, overflowing the native call stack and crashing the process.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether any caller or an enclosing WddxPacket state enforces a maximum recursion/nesting depth before this loop is reached.

findings.title: Scalar path XSS-safety depends entirely on unshown getWddxEncoded escaping
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of output (latent, delegated escaping)
findings.invariant: getWddxEncoded must XML-escape every attacker-controlled field it emits — both varValue and varName — so scalar serialization cannot inject markup.
findings.failure_mode: The scalar value and its name are passed to getWddxEncoded, whose body is not shown. Since the composite path in the same function escapes nothing, this helper is the only place escaping could occur; if it does not escape ', ", <, >, & then attacker-controlled scalar strings and their names inject markup identically to the composite-path sinks.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect getWddxEncoded to confirm it XML-escapes both the value body and the varName attribute for every WDDX type it emits.

audit_checklist: Confirm varName (line 12) is XML-entity-encoded before entering the single-quoted name attribute on the composite path.
Confirm the object class name (lines 29 and 55) is XML-escaped before being written into the <string> body — both paths.
Verify getWddxEncoded escapes ', \", <, >, & in both the value and the name for every WDDX type (line 73 sink).
Check for a bounded serialization/recursion depth guarding recursiveAddVar (line 40) against deeply nested inputs.
Confirm the .data()/.c_str() results at lines 12, 29, 55, 67, 69 are consumed within their full expression and never stored beyond the temporary's lifetime.
Determine the downstream consumer of m_packetString (browser render, XML parse, storage) to confirm the injection impact is XSS vs. XML corruption.
